title: Generalized Cylinder Limits of Ricci Flow Singularities
speaker: Natasa Sesum
abstract: We study multiply warped product geometries MN:=Bn×Fn1×···×FnAg = g_B + \sum_{a=1}^A v_a^2 g_{F^{n_a}} and show that for an open set of initial data within multiply warped product geometries the Ricci flow starting at any of those develops generalized cylinder as singularity model. More precisely, for any p and q we construct an open set of initial data within multiply warped product geometris whose Ricci flows develop S^p\times R^q as a singularity model.

proof_stages.label: Type-I Ricci-flow singularity models
proof_stages.summary: The speaker introduces Ricci flow, curvature blow-up, parabolic rescaling, ancient singularity models, gradient shrinking Ricci solitons, type-I singularities, essential blow-up sequences, and the Naber/Enders-Müller-Topping theorem giving nontrivial shrinker limits at type-I singular points.

proof_stages.label: Multiple warped-product reduction
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up multiple warped-product metrics with Einstein fibers, computes the relevant curvature components, and writes the Ricci-flow evolution equations in a gauged form suited for estimates.

proof_stages.label: Spatial-infinity type-I examples
proof_stages.summary: A noncompact theorem constructs complete type-I Ricci flows whose singularities occur at spatial infinity, showing that essential blow-up sequences need not converge to solitons unless they converge to an actual type-I singular point, with curvature control supplied by a noncompact maximum principle.

proof_stages.label: Stability motivation and compact-cylinder program
proof_stages.summary: Weak stability for cylindrical models is contrasted with much stronger mean-curvature-flow stability and genericity results, motivating the goal of finding open sets of compact warped-product initial data whose Ricci flows form generalized-cylinder singularities.

proof_stages.label: Open initial-data hypotheses
proof_stages.summary: Assumptions A1–A3 define the relevant open class of warped-product initial metrics by imposing single-fiber pinching, base-curvature control in dimension two, and scalar-curvature conditions sufficient for type-I behavior.

proof_stages.label: Base-one and base-two cylinder-limit theorems
proof_stages.summary: For one-dimensional base the theorem gives type-I singularities modeled on generalized cylinders S^p x R^q, while for two-dimensional base it gives type-I limits R^p x K^q with K a nonflat gradient shrinking Ricci soliton, leaving a classification problem for two-dimensional-base warped-product shrinkers.

proof_stages.label: Maximum-principle proof of type-I pinching and blow-up limits
proof_stages.summary: Maximum-principle estimates show that only the first fiber collapses, the other fibers and gradients remain controlled, base scalar curvature and Hessians are bounded appropriately, v_1^2 is comparable to T-t, and type-I rescalings have bounded curvature and converge to the stated shrinker limits.

proof_stages.label: Round-cylinder neck asymptotics and open problems
proof_stages.summary: The final part specializes to round-cylinder neck pinches, distinguishes nondegenerate and degenerate blow-up behavior, states a rotationally symmetric asymptotic trichotomy governed by Hermite modes of the linearized operator, and lists open problems on finiteness, dynamical stability, linear stability, and four-dimensional generic singularities.

examples.name: Positive scalar curvature forcing finite-time blow-up
examples.note: A metric with positive scalar curvature is used to make concrete how the quadratic reaction term in the scalar-curvature evolution can force a finite-time Ricci-flow singularity.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker writes the scalar-curvature evolution, bounds |Ric|^2 below by R^2/n, and uses the resulting differential inequality to explain finite-time blow-up for positive scalar curvature

examples.name: Rotationally symmetric dumbbell neck pinch
examples.note: Two large spheres joined by a thin neck illustrate a nondegenerate neck pinch whose parabolic blow-up is the round cylinder.
